paper_title: Entropy-Guided Adaptive Token Merging for Robust and Efficient Vision Transformers
email_title: Entropy-Guided Adaptive Token Merging for Robust and Efficient Vision Transformers

sections.summary: The paper studies the robustness of token-reduction techniques for Vision Transformers under distribution shift (ImageNet-C). It finds that aggressive token merging (e.g., ToMe with large merge ratios on DeiT-B) leads to disproportionately larger accuracy degradation under corruption compared to the full model. To mitigate this, it proposes Entropy-Guided Token Merging (EGTM), a training-free scheme that adapts the per-layer merge ratio using CLS-row attention entropy as a shift/difficulty signal. On DeiT-B with r=16, EGTM reportedly closes the robustness gap while still delivering a modest 1.15× throughput gain over the uncompressed model.
sections.strengths:
Technical novelty and innovation

The insight to use CLS-row attention entropy as a runtime indicator of input difficulty/shift to adapt token merging is simple, elegant, and training-free.
Per-layer adaptation via normalized entropy (z-scored against a small clean calibration set) is a pragmatic design choice that fits well with existing inference pipelines.
The work draws attention to a largely overlooked aspect of token reduction: robustness under distribution shift, and articulates a concrete metric (degradation gap) for “disproportionate” degradation.


Experimental rigor and validation

The paper evaluates multiple token reduction strategies (ToMe, random drop, training-free EViT-style) and provides per-corruption analyses on DeiT-B.
Ablations compare different entropy variants (CLS-only vs full attention; entropy vs variance), stride-based computation, importance protection, and hyperparameter sensitivity.
Throughput is measured with warmup and synchronization, and results report means and standard deviations over multiple calibration seeds for EGTM.


Clarity of presentation

The core method is clearly specified with formulas, calibration protocol, and a concrete per-layer adaptation rule with interpretable parameters (α, β).
The notion of “degradation gap” relative to a no-reduction baseline is well-defined and consistently used across experiments.
Limitations and scope are candidly discussed, including cases where EGTM underperforms (e.g., DeiT-S with modest merging ratios) and evaluation constraints.


Significance of contributions

Empirically highlights that token merging may degrade robustness disproportionately for larger models and aggressive merging ratios, a critical deployment consideration.
Offers a lightweight mitigation that can be integrated without retraining, potentially influencing practice in efficiency-oriented ViT deployments.
sections.weaknesses:
Technical limitations or concerns

The reliance on CLS-row attention entropy as the sole signal may be brittle in cases where entropy decreases under shift (e.g., brightness), or when attention dynamics do not align with input difficulty. This is acknowledged but not robustly addressed beyond adding a floor α.
Calibration appears to be performed with the baseline ToMe active, which may make the learned entropy statistics specific to the chosen base ratio and merging behavior, potentially hindering transferability across settings or backbones.


Experimental gaps or methodological issues

The evaluation uses a 5K subset of ImageNet val and only 12/15 ImageNet-C corruptions at severity 5; this is far from the community standard (50K, 15 corruptions, typically aggregated across all severities).
Comparisons omit strong adaptive baselines that do not require retraining but could provide alternative signals, e.g., softmax entropy or logit margin to modulate r; and they do not evaluate recent runtime-adaptive token merging via thresholding/BO (e.g., adaptive ToMe-like methods that adjust number of merges by similarity thresholds).
Only DeiT-S and DeiT-B are covered; no Swin/ViT-L or other families are evaluated, limiting generality.
The “training-free EViT-style” baseline likely underperforms fine-tuned EViT; without the fine-tuned counterpart the comparison is not entirely fair.


Clarity or presentation issues

In the entropy–severity analysis, the narrative occasionally confuses correlation coefficients (ρ) with p-values (e.g., “all 8 tested corruptions show positive p”); this should be corrected for statistical clarity.
Some figures appear as placeholders or with rendering artifacts; while not fundamental, they occasionally obscure exact quantitative details.


Missing related work or comparisons

Adaptive token-count methods like ReViT (token-length assigner) and relevance-based pruning frameworks like SPOT (attention dynamics) are not discussed in-depth or compared, even for qualitative positioning; while they require training, they are important context for adaptive-token computation and robustness.
Recent adaptive merging with per-layer similarity thresholds and BO for runtime selection (e.g., adaptive ToMe/ToFu-like via BO) has shown robustness to noise and would be a relevant point of comparison or discussion.
sections.detailed_comments:
Technical soundness evaluation

The core mechanism (entropy-guided rℓ) is technically sound and easy to implement. Monitoring CLS-row attention, already computed in attention layers, adds only O(N) per-head per-layer operations. The z-score normalization against clean calibration is reasonable.
Potential confounds: computing attention entropy for adaptation changes token counts in subsequent layers, which in turn affects later-layer attention distributions from those used in calibration. The authors implicitly handle this per-layer with normalization, but it would be helpful to quantify how sensitive calibration is to the base ratio r0 and to architectural changes (e.g., swapping backbones).
The decision to use only CLS-row entropy is well-motivated for global difficulty, but alternatives (e.g., averaging non-CLS entropies, head-wise gating, or including softmax-output entropy) could be competitive. The ablations test CLS vs full entropy but do not include logit-based uncertainty or token-similarity statistics as controls.


Experimental evaluation assessment

The main qualitative findings are compelling: substantial disproportionate degradation with aggressive token merging on DeiT-B, and a near-complete recovery via EGTM while retaining some speedup. The per-corruption breakdown is informative and shows consistent gains, especially under noise and digital corruptions.
However, the evaluation scope is narrow relative to community norms: 5K subset, 12/15 corruptions, single severity. Reporting standard ImageNet-C metrics (mCE/Relative mCE) across severities and the full 50K set would strengthen the claims considerably.
The throughput trade-off analysis is honest and practical (e.g., hooks overhead), but the claim of “<2%” overhead with stride-3 vs “~35%” with all layers needs more systematic measurement across models and batch sizes, and a clearer accounting relative to ToMe alone vs no reduction.
The robustness-importance scaling argument (larger model + higher r ⇒ larger gap) is plausible and partially supported by S vs B, but a third point (e.g., DeiT-L, Swin-B, or ViT-L/16) would make the trend more convincing.


Comparison with related work (using the summaries provided)

Prior surveys and studies (e.g., Hendrycks & Dietterich; ViT under shift) establish the benchmark landscape and the fact that robustness does not trivially scale with model size. This work contributes a targeted study on token reduction robustness, which seems novel.
Adaptive token-count methods like ReViT (training-time support for multiple token lengths + runtime assigner) and SPOT (fine-tuned pruning via attention dynamics) provide broader context: they demonstrate that token adaptivity can be learned. While EGTM is training-free and thus complementary, a brief empirical or stronger conceptual comparison would help position it among adaptive computation approaches, especially regarding robustness.
Recent adaptive merging with similarity thresholds and BO (runtime-adaptive τℓ) demonstrates noise robustness in edge-cloud settings and could be tested on ImageNet-C. Discussing how entropy-guided adaptation compares to threshold-guided policies (possibly with BO-calibrated τ) would enrich the narrative and may reveal hybrid strategies.


Discussion of broader impact and significance

The negative result—that aggressive token merging can be disproportionately brittle under shift—is important for practitioners and can influence deployment guidelines for efficient ViTs.
EGTM’s training-free, lightweight nature makes it attractive where retraining is infeasible; yet the current throughput overhead reduces its appeal on small/faster backbones. Improving engineering (strided evaluation, fused kernels) could make it broadly practical.
The paper is cautious about generalizability and failure modes (entropy decreases, calibration mismatch, adversarial manipulation to lower entropy). Exploring adversarial interactions (e.g., availability-oriented attacks on sparsification) would be valuable future work.
sections.questions_for_authors:
How sensitive is EGTM’s calibration to the base merging policy? Specifically, do you calibrate with no reduction vs fixed ToMe r0, and how does switching r0 or the backbone (e.g., Swin, ViT-L) affect δℓ distributions and performance?
Why not evaluate softmax-output entropy or logit margin as alternative difficulty signals for adaptive rℓ? These are cheaper to compute (no attention hooks) and may correlate with shift. Can you provide a control experiment?
Can you report full ImageNet-C (15 corruptions) with mCE and Relative mCE on the 50K set and multiple severities (preferably 1–5) for DeiT-B, at least for the main baselines (No Reduction, ToMe, EGTM)?
How does EGTM compare to an adaptive thresholding variant of ToMe that sets per-layer merge thresholds on cosine similarity (e.g., learned or BO-optimized τℓ) and thus adapts token count to input difficulty without attention entropy?
What is the memory and latency overhead breakdown for attention hooks and entropy computation across batch sizes and GPUs? Could a fused kernel or reading pre-softmax logits (before softmax) reduce the 35% overhead observed with all-layer hooks?
For corruptions like brightness where entropy decreases, have you tried using both tails (|δℓ|) or combining entropy with a confidence proxy to avoid missing “overconfidence” shifts?
Do the gains persist on other backbones (Swin-S/B, ViT-L/16) and other distribution-shift datasets (ImageNet-R, -Sketch, ImageNet-P)? Any preliminary observations?
Could EGTM be complementary to training-time robustness interventions (e.g., SATAs or augmentation strategies)? Have you tested combinations to see if adaptivity and training-time robustness add up?
sections.overall_assessment:
Estimated Score:
                    5.8/10
                    (Calibrated to ICLR scale)
                
            This paper tackles an important and underexplored issue: the robustness of token-reduction methods under distribution shift. The empirical observation that aggressive token merging disproportionately harms robustness on larger ViTs is valuable for the community. The proposed EGTM is a neat, training-free solution that leverages CLS-row attention entropy to adapt the merging ratio per layer at inference time. On DeiT-B, it convincingly mitigates the robustness gap while keeping a modest speedup, and the ablations suggest the core signal is effective and stable.
However, the experimental scope is limited compared to community standards (subset of ImageNet-C, partial corruption set, single severity), and key baselines for adaptive test-time policies (e.g., confidence/softmax entropy, similarity-threshold policies) are missing. Some statistical reporting around the entropy–severity analysis is imprecise, and throughput overheads on small models reduce practical impact in that regime. Despite these issues, the paper presents a timely and actionable finding with a simple method that can be adopted without retraining. With an expanded evaluation (full ImageNet-C and stronger adaptive baselines) and clarified statistical analyses, this could be a strong contribution. I lean toward a weak accept for the novelty of the robustness finding and the practicality of EGTM, conditional on addressing the main experimental gaps.
